name: Peck Morrison
abstract: John A. "Peck" Morrison (September 11, 1919 – February 25, 1988) was an American jazz bassist.

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: New Jazz at Carnegie
The New York Jazz Repertory Company: Duke Ellington 75th Birthday Salute
New York Jazz Repertory Company: A Musical Salute to Bud Freeman/ The Music of Fletcher Henderson

about_quotes.speaker: Lou Donaldson
about_quotes.evidence: I got Peck Morrison, bass player, who lived with me in the project. We were in the project. I got him on the date.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lou Donaldson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Sam Jones
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer states 'Peck Morrison was the bass player... in Tiny Bradshaw's band' and 'I saw some guys up there my age'. The interviewee (Sam Jones) does not claim a personal relationship, only that he saw him in the band.

about_quotes.speaker: Wilbur Ware
about_quotes.evidence: It turned out later that it was Peck Morrison's bass... Peck said, 'Man, I don't want to see the man in jail... I've seen Wilbur, and I did see him out in Chicago'
about_quotes.doc_title: 68088_Wilbur B. Ware, Sr oral history interview

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Wormworth
about_quotes.evidence: And through him I met Peck Morrison, the bassist, who was Lou’s bassist, man at the time, and we became close.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Wormworth interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 6, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Loren Schoenberg
about_quotes.evidence: a bass player I was working with named Peck Morrison who was in the Duke Ellington band
about_quotes.doc_title: Loren Schoenberg interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2019
